state_description: Waiting for human approval of the plan
product_description: Corporate expense management with receipt OCR, policy enforcement, approval workflows, accounting integration (QuickBooks, Xero, NetSuite), and corporate card program.

plan.description: Define business requirements for corporate expense management: expense submission flows, policy rule types, approval hierarchy models, accounting integration needs, and corporate card program scope.
acceptance_criteria: All user roles documented (employee, approver, finance admin, auditor)
Expense policy rule types enumerated (amount limits, category rules, per-diem rates, receipt requirements)
Approval workflow patterns defined (single, multi-level, amount-based routing)
Accounting field mapping documented for QuickBooks, Xero, and NetSuite
Corporate card program scope defined (virtual cards, physical cards, spend controls)

plan.description: Perform threat model and security review for expense management system. Produce SBOM, threat matrix, and PCI DSS scoping document for corporate card data flows.
acceptance_criteria: STRIDE threat model completed for all system components
PCI DSS cardholder data environment (CDE) scoped and documented
SBOM lists all third-party dependencies with license and CVE status
Penetration test plan covers API auth, card data endpoints, and OCR pipeline
SOC 2 trust service criteria mapped to system controls
PCI DSS SAQ completed
Encryption at rest and in transit

plan.description: Produce compliance artifacts for PCI DSS, SOC 2, and SOX: risk matrix, traceability matrix, data retention policy, audit trail requirements, and segregation of duties controls.
acceptance_criteria: Risk matrix covers all identified threats with likelihood and impact scores
Traceability matrix maps every compliance requirement to a system control
Data retention policy specifies receipt storage duration (7 years for SOX)
Audit trail spec requires immutable, timestamped records for all expense state changes
Segregation of duties matrix prevents submitter from also approving their own expenses

plan.description: Design user experience for expense submission, receipt capture, policy feedback, approval inbox, and finance dashboard. Produce wireframes and interaction specifications.
acceptance_criteria: Mobile-first receipt capture flow with camera and file upload options
Real-time policy feedback shown during expense entry (before submission)
Approval inbox shows expenses grouped by urgency and amount
Finance dashboard displays spend by category, department, and policy violation rate
All wireframes reviewed and approved by a representative from each user role

plan.description: Design and implement database schema for expense management: users, organizations, expenses, receipts, policies, approval workflows, accounting mappings, corporate cards, and audit log tables.

payload.encryption: Column-level encryption for card data (PAN, CVV references)

acceptance_criteria: All tables have created_at, updated_at, and deleted_at (soft delete) columns
Card PAN stored only as last-4 digits; full PAN never persisted (PCI DSS req 3)
Audit log table is append-only with no UPDATE/DELETE permissions granted to app role
Foreign key constraints enforce referential integrity across all relationships
Migration scripts run cleanly from empty schema to fully seeded dev database
Seed data includes 3 organizations, 10 users, sample policies, and 50 expense records
Transaction isolation level documented
Audit trail on all writes

plan.description: Build core backend service: authentication (OAuth2/SAML SSO), organization management, user/role management, expense CRUD, and audit logging middleware.
acceptance_criteria: All endpoints require authentication; unauthenticated requests return 401
Tenant isolation enforced at query layer — org A cannot read org B data
Expense state machine rejects invalid transitions (e.g., PAID → DRAFT)
Every state transition writes an immutable audit log entry with actor, timestamp, and previous state
SAML SSO login tested with a mock IdP (e.g., Okta dev account)
RBAC blocks employees from accessing admin endpoints; returns 403

plan.description: Build receipt OCR pipeline: upload endpoint, async OCR processing (Google Vision API or AWS Textract), structured data extraction (merchant, amount, date, currency, line items), and confidence scoring.

payload.storage: S3-compatible object store with server-side encryption

acceptance_criteria: Receipt upload returns a job_id within 200ms; OCR completes asynchronously
Extracted fields auto-populate expense form with confidence scores shown to user
Receipts with confidence < 0.85 flagged for manual review
Duplicate receipt detection (same merchant + amount + date within 30 days) triggers warning
Receipt images stored encrypted at rest; access via signed URLs expiring in 1 hour
OCR pipeline processes 100 concurrent receipts without queue backlog exceeding 30 seconds

plan.description: Build policy enforcement engine: configurable policy rules (amount limits, category restrictions, per-diem rates, receipt requirements, duplicate detection), real-time evaluation, and violation reporting.

payload.evaluation: rule engine evaluates on SUBMIT and in real-time during form entry

acceptance_criteria: Policy rules configurable via API without code changes
Policy evaluation returns results within 100ms for single expense
Violation severity (BLOCK/WARN/FLAG) correctly prevents or allows submission
Policy version history maintained; expenses evaluated against policy active at submission time
Per-diem rates configurable by country/city with effective date ranges
Policy changes require finance admin role; changes logged in audit trail

plan.description: Build approval workflow engine: configurable multi-level approval chains (by amount, department, category), parallel approvals, delegation, escalation on timeout, and email/Slack notifications.
acceptance_criteria: Approval chains configurable per department and expense category without code changes
Amount-based routing correctly escalates expenses above threshold to higher approver
Delegation persists approver's permissions for specified date range
Escalation fires after configurable timeout (default 48h); re-notifies manager's manager
Bulk approval allows approving up to 50 expenses in single action
All approval decisions (approve/reject/delegate) recorded in immutable audit log

plan.description: Build accounting integration layer: QuickBooks Online, Xero, and NetSuite connectors with OAuth, field mapping configuration, bi-directional sync, and error recovery.

payload.sync_strategy: event-driven push on APPROVED state + nightly reconciliation job
payload.error_handling: dead letter queue with retry and finance admin alerting

acceptance_criteria: OAuth flow for each provider completes and tokens stored encrypted
Approved expense exports to QuickBooks as Expense with correct account coding within 60 seconds
Approved expense exports to Xero as Bank Transaction with tracking categories
Approved expense exports to NetSuite as Expense Report with department and class
Field mapping UI allows finance admin to map expense categories to GL account codes
Failed exports land in dead letter queue with error details; auto-retry 3 times before alerting
Nightly reconciliation job detects and flags any approved-but-not-exported expenses

plan.description: Build corporate card program: virtual and physical card issuance via Marqeta or Stripe Issuing, spend controls, real-time transaction webhooks, auto-reconciliation to expenses, and cardholder management.

payload.pci_compliance: SAQ D — all card data flows through card provider; no PAN storage

acceptance_criteria: Virtual card issued within 5 seconds of request via API
Real-time transaction webhook received and processed within 10 seconds of card swipe
Card transaction auto-matched to open expense or creates pre-populated expense draft
Spend controls enforced at card provider level; over-limit transactions declined
Card freeze takes effect within 5 seconds of admin action
No PAN stored in application database; only last-4 digits and card provider token
Manager dashboard shows real-time team card spend by cardholder and category

plan.description: Build employee-facing web and mobile PWA: expense submission form with real-time policy feedback, receipt camera capture, OCR result review, expense list, and notification center.

payload.mobile: PWA with camera API for receipt capture
payload.offline_support: draft expenses cached locally, sync on reconnect

acceptance_criteria: Receipt photo taken via camera on mobile and uploaded within 3 taps
OCR-extracted fields auto-populate form; user can edit any field
Policy violations shown inline during form entry before submission
Expense list filters by status, date range, amount, and category
Offline draft saved to IndexedDB; submitted automatically when connection restored
PWA installable on iOS and Android; passes Lighthouse PWA audit score ≥ 90

plan.description: Build approver interface: approval inbox with expense details, receipt viewer, policy violation summary, bulk approval, delegation controls, and approval history.
acceptance_criteria: Approval inbox loads within 2 seconds with up to 200 pending expenses
Receipt viewable inline without leaving approval screen
Policy violations highlighted with specific rule violated and dollar over-limit
Bulk approve/reject applies to selected expenses with single confirmation
Rejection requires comment; comment stored in audit trail and emailed to submitter
Delegation wizard allows setting date range and selecting delegate from org directory

plan.description: Build finance admin dashboard: spend analytics, policy editor, accounting integration setup, corporate card management, export center, and compliance reports.
acceptance_criteria: Spend dashboard renders charts for current month within 3 seconds for orgs with 10k expenses
Policy editor allows creating, editing, and versioning rules with effective dates
Accounting integration wizard guides through OAuth and field mapping in under 10 minutes
Audit log viewer searchable by actor, expense ID, date range, and event type
Compliance report exportable as PDF with digital signature for SOX audit
Card management shows all active cards with spend-to-limit ratio and freeze control

plan.description: Implement AI-powered expense intelligence using the review_and_critique pattern: anomaly detection agent flags suspicious expenses, policy suggestion agent recommends policy updates based on violation patterns, and spend optimization agent surfaces savings opportunities.

agents.name: anomaly_detector
agents.role: Flags statistically anomalous expenses (outlier amounts, unusual merchants, velocity patterns)

agents.name: policy_optimizer
agents.role: Analyzes violation patterns and suggests policy rule adjustments to reduce friction while maintaining control

agents.name: spend_optimizer
agents.role: Identifies duplicate vendor spend, negotiation opportunities, and category consolidation recommendations

payload.hitl: All AI-generated flags and suggestions surface as proposals requiring finance admin review before any action

acceptance_criteria: Anomaly detector flags expenses > 3 standard deviations from employee's category baseline
Policy optimizer produces ranked list of policy changes with projected violation rate impact
Spend optimizer identifies top 10 vendors where consolidated buying could reduce cost
All agent proposals displayed in finance admin review queue; none execute automatically
Agent reasoning trace shown alongside each proposal for full explainability
False positive rate for anomaly detection < 5% measured over 30-day pilot

plan.description: Write comprehensive test suite: unit tests for policy engine and state machine, integration tests for accounting connectors and OCR pipeline, E2E tests for expense submission and approval flows.
acceptance_criteria: Policy engine unit tests cover all 8 rule types with boundary conditions
State machine tests verify all valid transitions and reject all invalid ones
Accounting integration tests run against sandbox/developer accounts for all three providers
E2E tests run in CI against a fully seeded test database
Test coverage report ≥ 85% on backend source
All tests pass in under 10 minutes in CI pipeline

plan.description: Set up infrastructure: Docker Compose for local dev, Kubernetes manifests for production, PostgreSQL with read replica, Redis for job queues, S3-compatible storage, and secrets management.

payload.secrets: AWS Secrets Manager or HashiCorp Vault
payload.networking: VPC with private subnets for database and worker tiers
payload.pci_network_segmentation: CDE isolated in separate subnet with strict ingress/egress rules

acceptance_criteria: docker compose up starts all services and passes health checks within 60 seconds
Kubernetes manifests deploy successfully to staging cluster
PostgreSQL read replica configured; read-heavy queries (analytics) routed to replica
Secrets never appear in environment variables or logs; all read from secrets manager at runtime
CDE subnet has no direct internet access; card data flows only through internal service mesh
Resource limits and pod disruption budgets set on all production deployments

plan.description: Set up CI/CD pipeline: GitHub Actions workflows for test, build, security scan (SAST/DAST/dependency audit), and deploy to staging and production with rollback capability.

payload.rollback: automated rollback if post-deploy smoke tests fail within 5 minutes
payload.branch_protection: require 2 approvals + all CI green before merge to main

acceptance_criteria: CI pipeline runs on every PR and completes in under 15 minutes
SAST scan with zero high or critical findings required to pass
Dependency audit blocks on known CVEs with CVSS ≥ 7.0
Production deploy requires manual approval gate in GitHub Actions
Automated rollback triggered and completed within 5 minutes of failed smoke test
Deploy logs stored for 90 days for SOX audit trail

plan.description: Execute security hardening: implement rate limiting, input sanitization, CSP headers, mTLS between services, PCI DSS control validation, and produce penetration test report for card data flows.

payload.pentest: automated DAST with OWASP ZAP + manual review of card data endpoints

acceptance_criteria: Rate limiting enforced: 100 req/min per IP, 1000 req/min per authenticated user
All API responses include HSTS, CSP, X-Content-Type-Options, and X-Frame-Options headers
mTLS configured between API service and worker service
OWASP ZAP scan produces zero high-severity findings on staging environment
PCI DSS requirements 2, 3, 6, 8, and 10 documented as satisfied with evidence
Penetration test report signed off by security reviewer and stored for audit
PCI DSS SAQ completed
Encryption at rest and in transit

plan.description: Produce final compliance package for SOC 2 readiness: control evidence collection, access control review, encryption attestation, incident response plan, and vendor risk assessments for OCR and card providers.
acceptance_criteria: All five SOC 2 common criteria addressed with at least one control per criterion
Vendor risk assessments completed for Marqeta/Stripe and Google Vision/Textract
Incident response plan defines RTO ≤ 4 hours and RPO ≤ 1 hour
Encryption attestation covers data at rest (AES-256) and in transit (TLS 1.3)
SOX financial controls matrix maps each control to the system feature enforcing it
Compliance package reviewed and approved by legal_advisor

plan.description: Produce technical and user documentation: API reference (OpenAPI), employee user guide, admin setup guide, accounting integration runbook, and architecture decision records.
acceptance_criteria: OpenAPI spec covers all endpoints with request/response schemas and error codes
Employee user guide covers expense submission, receipt capture, and status tracking
Accounting integration runbook includes step-by-step OAuth setup for each provider with screenshots
ADRs document key decisions: database choice, card provider selection, OCR provider, auth strategy
All docs reviewed by a non-engineer (product_manager) for clarity

flaws: Missing reimbursement payment flow entirely. The plan approves expenses and exports them to accounting, but never pays employees. ACH/wire disbursement is a core feature of any expense management product — employees cannot receive money. This is a fundamental product gap, not a backlog item.

Multi-currency is absent despite being an OCR extraction field. The plan extracts 'currency' from receipts but has no FX conversion logic, no exchange rate source, no policy enforcement in foreign currencies, and no accounting export in base currency. Any company with international employees hits this on day one.

PCI DSS SAQ D classification is almost certainly wrong. If the application never stores, processes, or transmits PANs (delegating all card data to Marqeta/Stripe), the correct scope is SAQ A or SAQ A-EP. SAQ D requires 329 controls. Mis-scoping upward means months of unnecessary compliance work; mis-scoping downward is an audit failure. This needs a QSA to determine scope before Step 2.

Xero OAuth tokens expire in 30 minutes; QuickBooks tokens in 60 minutes. The accounting integration (Step 10) mentions token storage but has no token refresh implementation or acceptance criterion. Production will fail silently after the first token expiry, blocking all accounting exports.

Real-time policy evaluation during form entry (Step 8) with no debouncing strategy will hammer the API on every keystroke. At 100 concurrent users editing forms, this is a self-inflicted DDoS. The acceptance criterion of 100ms evaluation time does not account for concurrent load or network latency on mobile.

NetSuite OAuth 1.0a (Token-Based Authentication) is significantly more complex than OAuth 2.0 — requires HMAC-SHA256 signing of each request, nonce management, and timestamp synchronization. The plan treats all three accounting integrations as equivalent effort. NetSuite alone could double Step 10's timeline.

Bulk approval of 50 expenses in a single action (Step 9) is a SOX control violation risk. SOX requires evidence of actual review, not just an approval click. There is no minimum-review-time control, no anti-rubber-stamp mechanism, and no auditor guidance on what constitutes a valid approval. An auditor will flag this.

iOS PWA camera capture is unreliable. iOS Safari restricts camera API access in PWA/homescreen mode on many iOS versions. The 'receipt capture within 3 taps' acceptance criterion will fail on a significant percentage of iPhone users. This requires a native wrapper (Capacitor/React Native) or explicit iOS Safari browser targeting — neither of which is planned.

Step 15 anomaly detection uses '3 standard deviations from baseline' but provides no cold-start solution. New employees have no baseline. New expense categories have no baseline. New organizations have no baseline. The false positive rate will be 100% for the first 30-90 days, destroying user trust before the feature can prove value.

Approval delegation (Step 9) has no chain delegation guard. If Alice delegates to Bob, and Bob can re-delegate to Carol, approval authority can be arbitrarily transferred across the org with a full audit trail of delegations but no policy check on whether Carol is authorized to approve Alice's expense tier. This is an internal control failure.

The audit log table has no tamper-evidence mechanism beyond 'no UPDATE/DELETE permissions.' A compromised app role, a DBA, or a PostgreSQL superuser can still modify records. SOX requires tamper-evident logs. The plan needs cryptographic chaining (hash of previous entry in each row) or an external append-only log service.

Step 6 implements SAML 2.0 SSO with acceptance criteria limited to 'tested with a mock IdP.' SAML has well-known implementation vulnerabilities: XML signature wrapping attacks, XXE, comment injection in NameID. A mock IdP test does not catch these. The plan needs a real IdP integration test AND a SAML-specific security review.
suggestions: Add Step 2.5 or amend Step 2: engage a PCI QSA for formal CDE scoping before any architecture is finalized. The card data flow determines whether this is SAQ A, A-EP, or D — a decision that changes the compliance workload by an order of magnitude.

Add an employee reimbursement step between Steps 9 and 10: ACH batch file generation, bank integration (via Dwolla, Modern Treasury, or direct ACH), payment status webhook handling, and reconciliation back to expense records. Without this, the product cannot complete its primary job.

Add multi-currency as a first-class concern to Step 5 (database: add base_currency, original_amount, exchange_rate, exchange_rate_date columns to expenses) and Step 8 (policy engine must compare amounts in base currency). Add FX rate provider (ECB, Open Exchange Rates, or Wise) to Step 6.

For real-time policy evaluation, implement client-side debouncing (300ms minimum) in Step 12, cache policy rules in Redis with a short TTL in Step 8, and add a dedicated policy evaluation rate limit separate from the general API rate limit.

Add cryptographic hash chaining to the audit_log table in Step 5: each row stores sha256(previous_row_hash + current_row_data). Add a nightly audit log integrity verification job. This is the difference between 'append-only by permission' and 'tamper-evident by design.'

For NetSuite, consider using a third-party connector library (e.g., netsuite-python) rather than building OAuth 1.0a from scratch. Estimate NetSuite integration at 2-3x the effort of QuickBooks/Xero and reflect this in resource planning.

For anomaly detection cold-start (Step 15), implement a peer-group baseline as fallback: if an employee has <10 expenses in a category, use the department median. Document this in the acceptance criteria with a minimum sample size threshold.

Add email infrastructure as an explicit step (or sub-step of Step 9): SES/SendGrid setup, transactional email templates, unsubscribe handling (CAN-SPAM/GDPR), and bounce/complaint processing. Email is load-bearing for approval notifications and rejection reasons.

For JWT storage, specify httpOnly SameSite=Strict cookies rather than localStorage in Step 6. This eliminates an entire class of XSS token theft. Update the acceptance criteria to include a cookie security audit.

Add GDPR/CCPA compliance artifacts to Step 3 or Step 20. Employee expense receipts contain purchase location data, meal companion names, and personal spending patterns. Any EU or California employees trigger these regulations. At minimum: data subject access request flow, right-to-erasure conflict with 7-year SOX retention (these must be reconciled), and a privacy notice.
missing: Employee reimbursement payment processing: ACH disbursement, payment status tracking, failed payment handling, and bank account management for employees.
Multi-currency support: FX rate ingestion, currency conversion at submission time, base-currency normalization for policy enforcement, and accounting export in company base currency.
Email infrastructure: transactional email provider setup, templates for approval requests, rejection notices, escalations, and compliance with CAN-SPAM/GDPR unsubscribe requirements.

GDPR and CCPA compliance: data subject rights implementation, privacy notice, retention conflict resolution (GDPR erasure vs. SOX 7-year requirement), and data processing agreements with OCR and card providers.
Disaster recovery testing: the plan documents RTO/RPO targets in Step 20 but has no step for actually testing failover, backup restoration, or the DR runbook.

Card issuing bank partnership: Marqeta and Stripe Issuing both require approval of the business as a program manager. This is a business/legal prerequisite with weeks-to-months lead time that has no planned step.

Mobile push notifications: approval requests via push are more reliable than email for time-sensitive approvals. The notification system (Step 9) covers email, Slack, and in-app but not mobile push (APNs/FCM), which is load-bearing for a mobile-first PWA.

Expense receipt PAN scrubbing: OCR on receipts will extract visible card numbers (many paper receipts show partial PANs). The OCR pipeline has no step to detect and scrub any card-number-pattern text from extracted data before storage.
Accounting token refresh strategy: explicit token refresh logic, encrypted token rotation, and alerting when refresh fails — not implied by 'store tokens encrypted.'

Data retention enforcement mechanism: the policy says 7-year receipt retention for SOX, but there is no scheduled job, storage lifecycle policy, or legal hold mechanism to enforce it. Policy documentation is not the same as enforcement.

Webhook signature verification: Marqeta and Stripe both provide HMAC signatures on webhook payloads. There is no acceptance criterion requiring verification of these signatures, leaving the card transaction endpoint open to spoofed transactions.
security_risks: SAML XML signature wrapping: python3-saml and similar libraries have had CVEs for signature wrapping attacks where a valid signed assertion is cloned into an unsigned position. The acceptance criterion 'tested with mock IdP' does not exercise this attack surface. Require a SAML security review against OWASP SAML Security Cheat Sheet before Step 6 ships.

Webhook spoofing on card transaction endpoint (Step 11): without HMAC signature verification and timestamp replay protection on Marqeta/Stripe webhooks, any party who discovers the webhook URL can inject fraudulent card transactions that auto-create expense records. This is a direct financial fraud vector.

OCR pipeline PAN exposure: receipts frequently display partial or full card numbers. Unguarded OCR extraction stores whatever text is in the image. A malicious employee could submit a receipt image containing a PAN in a format that bypasses pattern detection. The extracted text must be scanned for PAN patterns (Luhn check) and scrubbed before storage.

Accounting OAuth token scope over-provisioning: QuickBooks and Xero OAuth scopes, if not minimized, grant read/write access to the entire organization's financial data. A compromised token gives an attacker full accounting access. The plan has no acceptance criterion enforcing minimum-scope OAuth requests.

Delegated approval privilege escalation: if delegation is implemented as 'copy approver permissions to delegate,' an employee with no approval authority could be delegated authority over their own manager's expenses if the manager delegates generically. The delegation model needs explicit constraints: a delegate can only approve expenses the delegator could approve, and cannot approve expenses from the delegator.

JWT in localStorage (implied default): if the React frontend stores JWT access tokens in localStorage, any XSS vulnerability — including those in third-party dependencies — exfiltrates tokens. The plan adds CSP headers but does not specify token storage mechanism. CSP reduces XSS risk; it does not eliminate it.

mTLS certificate lifecycle gap: Step 19 requires mTLS between services but has no acceptance criterion for certificate rotation, revocation, or expiry alerting. An expired internal certificate silently breaks service-to-service communication in production with no user-visible error until a cascading failure.

Bulk approval CSRF: the bulk approve endpoint (Step 9) processes up to 50 financial approvals per request. Without SameSite cookie enforcement and CSRF token validation, a cross-site request could trigger mass approvals from an authenticated approver's session. This is a high-value CSRF target.
final_review.summary: This is a structurally sound, well-sequenced plan that correctly identifies the major components of a corporate expense management system and includes compliance steps rarely seen in MVP plans. The dependency graph is mostly logical, the database schema is thorough, and the decision to front-load security and compliance (Steps 2-3) before implementation is correct. However, the plan has a fundamental product gap: it approves and exports expenses but never pays employees. ACH reimbursement is not a stretch goal — it is the reason the product exists. Multi-currency is similarly absent despite being extraction-targeted in the OCR step. On the compliance side, PCI DSS SAQ scope is likely mis-classified (SAQ D adds hundreds of controls that may be unnecessary), GDPR/CCPA are unaddressed for a system storing employee personal spending data, and the audit log's tamper-evidence relies entirely on database permissions rather than cryptographic guarantees a SOX auditor will accept. The iOS PWA camera limitation is a known platform constraint that will break the core mobile receipt capture flow on a large portion of the target user base. Accounting OAuth token refresh is a silent production failure waiting to happen on day two. These are not backlog items — they are gaps that prevent the system from functioning correctly in production. Score reflects that the plan's architecture and compliance intent are strong but the execution would stall on payment processing, currency handling, and several security controls before reaching a releasable state.

Card issuing bank partnership: Marqeta and Stripe Issuing both require approval of the business as a program manager. This is a business/legal prerequisite with weeks-to-months lead time that has no planned step.

Mobile push notifications: approval requests via push are more reliable than email for time-sensitive approvals. The notification system (Step 9) covers email, Slack, and in-app but not mobile push (APNs/FCM), which is load-bearing for a mobile-first PWA.

Expense receipt PAN scrubbing: OCR on receipts will extract visible card numbers (many paper receipts show partial PANs). The OCR pipeline has no step to detect and scrub any card-number-pattern text from extracted data before storage.
Accounting token refresh strategy: explicit token refresh logic, encrypted token rotation, and alerting when refresh fails — not implied by 'store tokens encrypted.'

Data retention enforcement mechanism: the policy says 7-year receipt retention for SOX, but there is no scheduled job, storage lifecycle policy, or legal hold mechanism to enforce it. Policy documentation is not the same as enforcement.

Webhook signature verification: Marqeta and Stripe both provide HMAC signatures on webhook payloads. There is no acceptance criterion requiring verification of these signatures, leaving the card transaction endpoint open to spoofed transactions.
security_risks: SAML XML signature wrapping: python3-saml and similar libraries have had CVEs for signature wrapping attacks where a valid signed assertion is cloned into an unsigned position. The acceptance criterion 'tested with mock IdP' does not exercise this attack surface. Require a SAML security review against OWASP SAML Security Cheat Sheet before Step 6 ships.

Webhook spoofing on card transaction endpoint (Step 11): without HMAC signature verification and timestamp replay protection on Marqeta/Stripe webhooks, any party who discovers the webhook URL can inject fraudulent card transactions that auto-create expense records. This is a direct financial fraud vector.

OCR pipeline PAN exposure: receipts frequently display partial or full card numbers. Unguarded OCR extraction stores whatever text is in the image. A malicious employee could submit a receipt image containing a PAN in a format that bypasses pattern detection. The extracted text must be scanned for PAN patterns (Luhn check) and scrubbed before storage.

Accounting OAuth token scope over-provisioning: QuickBooks and Xero OAuth scopes, if not minimized, grant read/write access to the entire organization's financial data. A compromised token gives an attacker full accounting access. The plan has no acceptance criterion enforcing minimum-scope OAuth requests.

Delegated approval privilege escalation: if delegation is implemented as 'copy approver permissions to delegate,' an employee with no approval authority could be delegated authority over their own manager's expenses if the manager delegates generically. The delegation model needs explicit constraints: a delegate can only approve expenses the delegator could approve, and cannot approve expenses from the delegator.

JWT in localStorage (implied default): if the React frontend stores JWT access tokens in localStorage, any XSS vulnerability — including those in third-party dependencies — exfiltrates tokens. The plan adds CSP headers but does not specify token storage mechanism. CSP reduces XSS risk; it does not eliminate it.

mTLS certificate lifecycle gap: Step 19 requires mTLS between services but has no acceptance criterion for certificate rotation, revocation, or expiry alerting. An expired internal certificate silently breaks service-to-service communication in production with no user-visible error until a cascading failure.

Bulk approval CSRF: the bulk approve endpoint (Step 9) processes up to 50 financial approvals per request. Without SameSite cookie enforcement and CSRF token validation, a cross-site request could trigger mass approvals from an authenticated approver's session. This is a high-value CSRF target.
history.summary: This is a structurally sound, well-sequenced plan that correctly identifies the major components of a corporate expense management system and includes compliance steps rarely seen in MVP plans. The dependency graph is mostly logical, the database schema is thorough, and the decision to front-load security and compliance (Steps 2-3) before implementation is correct. However, the plan has a fundamental product gap: it approves and exports expenses but never pays employees. ACH reimbursement is not a stretch goal — it is the reason the product exists. Multi-currency is similarly absent despite being extraction-targeted in the OCR step. On the compliance side, PCI DSS SAQ scope is likely mis-classified (SAQ D adds hundreds of controls that may be unnecessary), GDPR/CCPA are unaddressed for a system storing employee personal spending data, and the audit log's tamper-evidence relies entirely on database permissions rather than cryptographic guarantees a SOX auditor will accept. The iOS PWA camera limitation is a known platform constraint that will break the core mobile receipt capture flow on a large portion of the target user base. Accounting OAuth token refresh is a silent production failure waiting to happen on day two. These are not backlog items — they are gaps that prevent the system from functioning correctly in production. Score reflects that the plan's architecture and compliance intent are strong but the execution would stall on payment processing, currency handling, and several security controls before reaching a releasable state.
